comparator_reference_group: Overall cohort; flu subset vs other viral pneumonia within cohort
finding_text: Flu represented 14% of viral pneumonia cases and contributed to severe ICU burden
exposure_transmission_context: New imaging infiltrates plus sepsis used as deterioration context; ICU-acquired pneumonia setting
predictors_risk_factors: Post-surgical status; COPD; oncology immunosuppression
labs_vitals_radiology: ANC <500 associated with 9.1x risk; imaging infiltrates mentioned
environmental_structural_factors: ICU setting only; no detailed ward architecture reported
clinical_outcomes: High mortality; required MV mirroring overall cohort; prolonged ICU/hospital stay
operational_burden: HAP added 9.6 days to hospital stay and 4.5 days to ICU stay; rapid multiplexing demand
differential_diagnosis_coinfection: Overlap with Gram-negative ICU bacteria; Klebsiella and Pseudomonas common
seasonal_temporal_pattern: Monsoon peak July-September; year-round baseline risk
effect_sizes_model_performance: HR 9.0 for pneumonia death overall; VAP 18.7/1000 days
adjustment_level: Not clear from summary if multivariable for all findings
key_limitations: Highly specific oncology ICU population; flu-stratified details limited
study_reported_implication: Severe nosocomial respiratory infection in oncology ICU is strongly associated with immunosuppression, neutropenia, ventilation burden, and prolonged stay
codeblue_translation: Good for high-risk oncology ward logic and seasonal preparedness, but not a clean influenza-only paper

hospital_acquired_definition: Nosocomial acquisition post-admission; associated with MV risk

finding_text: AH1N1 36.8% and H3N2 26.9%; paradoxical breathing flagged as key flu deterioration sign

comparator_reference_group: HA-flu vs CA-flu; mixed HA-RVI comparisons
finding_text: Flu 6.6%; HA-flu associated with higher MV/RRT and higher in-hospital death than CA-flu; 2x more likely in cancer patients
exposure_transmission_context: New onset on oncology/SCT ward indicates acquisition context
predictors_risk_factors: Cancer 24.3%; chemotherapy within 30 days; SCT within 1 year
clinical_outcomes: High dialysis and ventilation demand; increased stay duration; higher in-hospital death vs CA-flu
differential_diagnosis_coinfection: Bacterial overlap increased severity
seasonal_temporal_pattern: Year-round threat in high-risk SCT patients
effect_sizes_model_performance: p<0.05 for higher MV/RRT; p<0.01 for higher in-hospital death vs CA-flu
adjustment_level: Adjusted status not fully clear from summary
key_limitations: Single-center high-risk population likely limits generalizability
study_reported_implication: Hospital-acquired respiratory viral infection is particularly severe in cancer/SCT populations and worse than community-acquired influenza in key outcomes
codeblue_translation: Strong support for ward vulnerability and high-risk oncology/SCT surveillance logic

diagnostic_method: Machine learning with SHAP and neural nets
comparator_reference_group: 975 flu cases screened vs 1224 COVID cases screened
finding_text: Flu cohort included in DRP-risk modeling
exposure_transmission_context: Hospital-acquired DRP acquisition in already ill viral inpatients
predictors_risk_factors: Age >66; indwelling catheters; MV use; LOS
labs_vitals_radiology: Albumin <35 g/L; low cholinesterase; SHAP top variables CHE, albumin, age
clinical_outcomes: DRP group had universal deterioration in study framing; DRPs linked to mortality and severity
differential_diagnosis_coinfection: High antibiotic use drives resistance; P. aeruginosa 16.4%; K. pneumoniae 16.1%
effect_sizes_model_performance: SHAP ranking identified top variables; neural nets used; exact AUC not in source summary
key_limitations: Focuses on DRPs rather than pure influenza outcomes; summary lacks full performance details
study_reported_implication: Hospital-acquired drug-resistant pathogens in viral respiratory disease can be predicted from routine clinical variables and materially worsen severity
codeblue_translation: Very useful for stewardship and bacterial-overlap module linked to influenza pathways

finding_text: Flu/pneumonia accounted for 28.9% of HA-sepsis and had OR 1.86 for death
exposure_transmission_context: Sepsis after surgery/device or during admission
predictors_risk_factors: Underlying multimorbidity; Medical Card proxy; cardiac disease for non-flu comparison
clinical_outcomes: 46.9% required critical care; 24.4% mortality; median stay 27d; ICU 9d
differential_diagnosis_coinfection: Secondary bacterial sepsis high risk

study_reported_implication: HA-sepsis due to respiratory viral syndromes carries major mortality and critical care burden at national scale

comparator_reference_group: Culture vs rapid multiplex PCR; unsuspected viral cases
finding_text: 4 unsuspected influenza cases detected (3 Influenza A, 1 Influenza B)
exposure_transmission_context: Culture-negative but clinically failing patients as trigger
predictors_risk_factors: NR
labs_vitals_radiology: Antibiotics masked 43% of cultures; rapid PCR identified pathogens in 1.5h vs 72h
clinical_outcomes: 100% ventilation cohort; overall mortality 21.7%; median ICU 11d; total stay 21.5d
differential_diagnosis_coinfection: Co-circulation with S. aureus and K. pneumoniae; antibiotic adjustment in 62%
effect_sizes_model_performance: 1.5h vs 72h; 43% cultures masked; antibiotics adjusted in 62%
key_limitations: Small ICU cohort; not influenza-only
study_reported_implication: Rapid multiplex PCR uncovers hidden influenza and other viral etiologies in ventilated HAP/VAP patients and materially changes stewardship
codeblue_translation: Excellent support for rapid testing, hidden influenza detection, and stewardship workflow

comparator_reference_group: Seasonal influenza periods vs pandemic periods
finding_text: Influenza did not trigger heightened vigilance or equivalent ICU escalation and showed no significant resistance impact
exposure_transmission_context: Healthcare worker behavior, burnout, shortages, and capacity limits influenced transmission-control response
predictors_risk_factors: Staff non-compliance and fear-based behavior differences were key operational predictors
labs_vitals_radiology: Hand hygiene 70%; glove non-compliance 40%; no major behavior change during routine influenza periods
environmental_structural_factors: Environmental cleaning and PPE intensified during pandemic response
clinical_outcomes: No equivalent ICU escalation during seasonal influenza; secondary infection burden rose in pandemic response
operational_burden: High PPE use and intensified environmental cleaning during pandemic response
differential_diagnosis_coinfection: Altered bacterial spread: CRE up, MRSA down
seasonal_temporal_pattern: Winter seasons Dec-Feb vs pandemic waves Mar-Nov
effect_sizes_model_performance: p=0.233 for no resistance impact in seasonal period; p=0.006 HA-BSI increase; p<0.001 MRSA decline
key_limitations: Not an influenza-outcome paper per se; behavior/institutional response focus
study_reported_implication: Response to routine influenza threats is weaker than to novel respiratory threats, with implications for compliance and automated enforcement
codeblue_translation: Important for governance logic and why automation is needed for routine respiratory threats

hospital_acquired_definition: Hospital-acquired symptom onset plus positive PCR >=3 days post-admission
comparator_reference_group: 868 HA-influenza vs 2901 HA-Omicron
finding_text: HA-influenza had acute complications 35.3%, ICU 2.6%, IMCU 1.4%, mortality 6.1%
exposure_transmission_context: Established inpatient respiratory symptoms with hospital-onset testing
predictors_risk_factors: Older age, higher Charlson score; lack of vaccination linked to prolonged stay
clinical_outcomes: Mortality 6.1% vs 6.2%; median stay 11 days for HA-flu; substantial acute complications
operational_burden: High critical care demand similar to Omicron
differential_diagnosis_coinfection: Secondary bacterial pneumonia frequent; bacterial coinfection not main focus
seasonal_temporal_pattern: Temperate winter seasonality for flu; year-round pandemic context
effect_sizes_model_performance: sHR 0.91 for mortality equivalence framing; Fine-Gray models
key_limitations: Observational nationwide cohort; pathogen-specific comparability may still have residual confounding
study_reported_implication: Modern hospital-acquired influenza remains as clinically serious as HA-COVID in several key outcomes
codeblue_translation: Very strong modern justification that HA-influenza deserves high-severity operational treatment
